quiz.question: The museum displayed a replica of the Rosetta Stone. A scholar needs to study the actual ancient carvings. Should she examine the museum exhibit?
options: Yes, a replica is identical to the original
No, she needs the genuine original
Only if the replica is very old
It depends on the museum's rating
quiz.answer: No, she needs the genuine original
quiz.explanation:
A replica is an exact copy — not the original itself.
• replica = a reproduction or copy of something
• A replica looks identical but lacks the authenticity of the source
• For scholarly work on actual ancient carvings, only the original will do

quiz.question: Spain launched an armada against England in 1588. What did England have to defend against?
options: A single powerful warship
A large fleet of warships
A land-based army
A team of spies
quiz.answer: A large fleet of warships
quiz.explanation:
An armada is a large fleet of warships.
• armada = a large naval force
• The Spanish Armada comprised around 130 ships
• From Spanish: 'armed force'

quiz.question: Item 4 on the meeting agenda was 'HR updates.' Which topic came fourth in the meeting?
options: The most urgent topic raised by attendees
HR updates
Whatever the chair decided on the day
The topic with the most votes
quiz.answer: HR updates
quiz.explanation:
An agenda is a pre-set list of topics to be covered in order at a meeting.
• agenda = an ordered list of planned discussion items
• Item 4 means it is addressed fourth, not earlier or later
• 'Hidden agenda' = a secret, undisclosed motive

quiz.question: The conductor announced a coda at the end of the symphony. At what point in the performance did the audience hear it?
options: At the very start
Halfway through
Just before the main theme
Right at the end
quiz.answer: Right at the end
quiz.explanation:
A coda is a concluding musical (or literary) passage — it always comes last.
• coda = a final, closing section
• From Italian: 'tail'
• In writing/film, a coda is an epilogue-like closing

quiz.question: The health guru claimed that drinking lemon water every morning was a panacea. What was he claiming it could do?
options: Cure one specific vitamin deficiency
Fix all health problems
Replace surgery
Boost energy only
quiz.answer: Fix all health problems
quiz.explanation:
A panacea is a supposed cure-all — something claimed to solve every problem.
• panacea = a universal remedy or fix-all
• Usually used skeptically — the claim sounds too good to be true
• From Greek: 'pan' (all) + 'akos' (remedy)

quiz.question: The journalist called the corruption trial 'a saga that had gripped the nation for three years.' What does this tell us about the trial?
options: It was a quick, simple case
It was a long and complex unfolding story
It involved only two people
It ended without a verdict
quiz.answer: It was a long and complex unfolding story
quiz.explanation:
A saga is a long, drawn-out series of events, often dramatic or difficult.
• saga = a lengthy, complex narrative or sequence of events
• Originally Old Norse epic tales of heroes
• Modern use: 'the whole visa saga took months'

quiz.question: A historian described the diary as apocrypha — interesting, but not usable as a primary source. Why can't she use it as solid evidence?
options: It is too recent
Its authenticity and authorship are doubtful
It is written in a foreign language
It is too short
quiz.answer: Its authenticity and authorship are doubtful
quiz.explanation:
Apocrypha refers to writings of uncertain or disputed authenticity.
• apocrypha = texts whose origin or authorship cannot be verified
• Adjective: apocryphal ('an apocryphal story' = probably not true)
• From Greek: 'hidden, obscure'

quiz.question: Leo has a phobia of spiders. He sees a tiny, harmless house spider on the wall. How is he most likely to react?
options: He calmly removes it
He feels intense fear, despite knowing it is harmless
He is mildly annoyed
He ignores it completely
quiz.answer: He feels intense fear, despite knowing it is harmless
quiz.explanation:
A phobia is an extreme, irrational fear that persists regardless of actual danger.
• phobia = an intense, disproportionate, irrational fear
• The key is irrational — the fear is not proportional to real threat
• From Greek: 'phobos' (fear)

quiz.question: Anna has acrophobia. Which of these activities would most directly trigger her condition?
options: Swimming in the ocean
Standing on the roof of a skyscraper
Entering a dark tunnel
Being in a crowded elevator
quiz.answer: Standing on the roof of a skyscraper
quiz.explanation:
Acrophobia is specifically the fear of heights.
• acrophobia = fear of heights ('acro-' = topmost point)
• A skyscraper rooftop is a direct height trigger
• Related: acrobat, acropolis — all involve high places

quiz.question: The landlord asked his tenant to repaint the fascia of the shop. The tenant painted the interior walls instead. Did he follow instructions?
options: Yes, the fascia is the interior wall
No, the fascia is the flat board on the outside front of the building
Yes, fascia means all paintable surfaces
No, the fascia is the roof
quiz.answer: No, the fascia is the flat board on the outside front of the building
quiz.explanation:
A fascia is the flat horizontal board on the exterior front of a building, above the shopfront.
• fascia = the signboard panel on the outside of a shopfront
• Also: a connective tissue layer in anatomy; a dashboard panel in cars
• From Latin: 'band, strip'

quiz.question: A student wants a brief but reliable overview of the French Revolution. Which source best fits the purpose of an encyclopedia?
options: A novel set during the French Revolution
A concise factual summary covering key events, dates, and figures
A personal memoir by a historian
A satirical cartoon from the era
quiz.answer: A concise factual summary covering key events, dates, and figures
quiz.explanation:
An encyclopedia is a reference work providing comprehensive summaries of knowledge across many subjects.
• encyclopedia = a reference book (or website) with informational summaries
• Designed for breadth and accessibility, not deep original argument
• From Greek: 'enkyklios paideia' — 'circular/general education'

quiz.question: Hearing her old school song filled Maria with nostalgia. What emotion was she most likely experiencing?
options: Excitement about the future
A bittersweet longing for the past
Anger at old memories
Boredom
quiz.answer: A bittersweet longing for the past
quiz.explanation:
Nostalgia is a sentimental, often bittersweet longing for the past.
• nostalgia = fond, wistful longing for earlier times
• From Greek: 'nostos' (homecoming) + 'algos' (pain)
• Originally meant homesickness; now means longing for any past experience

quiz.question: A young boy with hemophilia cuts his finger. Why does this require immediate medical attention, even though the cut is small?
options: He is likely to feel no pain
His blood does not clot properly, so bleeding is hard to stop
The cut may become infected immediately
He has unusually thin skin
quiz.answer: His blood does not clot properly, so bleeding is hard to stop
quiz.explanation:
Hemophilia is a genetic disorder in which blood lacks the ability to clot normally.
• hemophilia = inability of blood to clot, causing prolonged bleeding
• Even minor wounds can become life-threatening
• From Greek: 'haima' (blood) + 'philia' (tendency toward)

quiz.question: The doctor told Tom he had anemia and that his fatigue was a direct result of it. What is most likely causing his tiredness?
options: Too much iron in the blood
His blood is not carrying enough oxygen around his body
He has a high fever
He is dehydrated
quiz.answer: His blood is not carrying enough oxygen around his body
quiz.explanation:
Anemia is a condition in which red blood cells or hemoglobin are insufficient, reducing oxygen delivery.
• anemia = deficiency of red blood cells or hemoglobin
• Less oxygen to tissues → fatigue, weakness, paleness
• From Greek: 'an-' (without) + 'haima' (blood)

quiz.question: After winning the championship, football mania swept the country. What does this describe?
options: A mild interest in football
A widespread, intense obsession with football
A single fan's excitement
A government sports program
quiz.answer: A widespread, intense obsession with football
quiz.explanation:
Mania describes an extreme enthusiasm, obsession, or craze — often widespread.
• mania = an intense, uncontrolled enthusiasm or obsession
• In psychology: a state of abnormally elevated mood/energy
• Common suffix: Beatlemania, tulip mania

quiz.question: The dictator, convinced he was destined to rule the entire world, exhibited classic megalomania. What does this suggest about him?
options: He had modest, realistic goals
He had an obsessive, delusional craving for power and greatness
He was afraid of responsibility
He was focused on economic reform
quiz.answer: He had an obsessive, delusional craving for power and greatness
quiz.explanation:
Megalomania is an obsession with grandiose schemes or one's own power and importance.
• megalomania = delusional belief in one's own greatness or power
• From Greek: 'megas' (great) + 'mania' (obsession)
• A megalomaniac overestimates their importance and seeks unlimited control

quiz.question: Captain Ahab in Moby-Dick was consumed by monomania. What does this tell us about his mental state?
options: He had many competing obsessions
He was obsessively fixated on one single thing — the white whale
He had no strong feelings about anything
He was rational and focused
quiz.answer: He was obsessively fixated on one single thing — the white whale
quiz.explanation:
Monomania is an obsessive preoccupation with one single idea or subject.
• monomania = extreme focus on a single thought, goal, or grievance
• 'mono-' = one; the narrowness is the defining feature
• Ahab's pursuit of the whale to the exclusion of all else is the textbook example

quiz.question: The suspect was diagnosed with pyromania. What behavior would investigators most expect to find in his history?
options: An obsession with explosives
A compulsive urge to set fires
A fear of fire
A hobby of cooking over open flames
quiz.answer: A compulsive urge to set fires
quiz.explanation:
Pyromania is a compulsive urge to deliberately start fires.
• pyromania = an impulse-control disorder involving fire-setting
• From Greek: 'pyr' (fire) + 'mania' (obsession)
• Distinct from arson for profit — the motivation is psychological compulsion

quiz.question: A Victorian doctor described his patient as suffering from dipsomania. What was the patient's problem?
options: An addiction to gambling
An uncontrollable craving for alcohol
An obsessive fear of water
A compulsion to steal
quiz.answer: An uncontrollable craving for alcohol
quiz.explanation:
Dipsomania is an uncontrollable craving for alcohol — an older medical term for alcoholism.
• dipsomania = compulsive, uncontrollable urge to drink alcohol
• From Greek: 'dipsa' (thirst) + 'mania'
• Now largely replaced clinically by 'alcohol use disorder'

quiz.question: The florist recommended gardenias for the wedding centerpiece, warning that they bruise easily and need careful handling. What kind of item are gardenias?
options: A type of decorative vase
A fragrant flower
A tropical fruit
A climbing vine
quiz.answer: A fragrant flower
quiz.explanation:
A gardenia is a fragrant flowering plant with white or yellow blossoms.
• gardenia = a tropical/subtropical shrub known for its highly fragrant white flowers
• Named after Scottish botanist Alexander Garden
• Widely used in perfumery and ornamental arrangements

quiz.question: The soldier's uniform bore the insignia of a general. What did this tell observers about him?
options: His personal fashion choices
His rank and branch of service
His years of service only
The country he was born in
quiz.answer: His rank and branch of service
quiz.explanation:
Insignia are badges, emblems, or symbols that indicate rank, office, or membership.
• insignia = distinguishing emblems or badges of authority/rank
• From Latin: 'insignia' = marks, signs
• Examples: military rank badges, school crests, national emblems

quiz.question: After weeks of insomnia, David finally saw a doctor. What was his main complaint?
options: He was sleeping too much
He could not fall or stay asleep
He had vivid nightmares every night
He sleepwalked frequently
quiz.answer: He could not fall or stay asleep
quiz.explanation:
Insomnia is the inability to fall asleep or stay asleep regularly.
• insomnia = chronic difficulty sleeping
• From Latin: 'in-' (not) + 'somnus' (sleep)
• Distinct from nightmares or sleepwalking — the problem is getting sleep at all

quiz.question: Despite no evidence whatsoever, Gary was convinced his colleagues were plotting to get him fired. His therapist said this was paranoia. What does that mean?
options: He had valid concerns worth investigating
He suffered from irrational, unfounded suspicion and distrust of others
He was too trusting of his colleagues
He had a realistic assessment of workplace politics
quiz.answer: He suffered from irrational, unfounded suspicion and distrust of others
quiz.explanation:
Paranoia is an irrational and persistent belief that others are conspiring against you.
• paranoia = unfounded distrust, suspicion, or belief in persecution
• From Greek: 'para' (beside, beyond) + 'noos' (mind)
• Key word: irrational — it exists without real evidence

quiz.question: The autumn festival table was described as a cornucopia of seasonal produce. What does this suggest about the table?
options: There was a shortage of food
There was an overflowing abundance of food
The food was arranged in a horn-shaped bowl only
Only one type of food was served
quiz.answer: There was an overflowing abundance of food
quiz.explanation:
A cornucopia means an overflowing, abundant supply of good things.
• cornucopia = an abundance; a great variety of something
• From Latin: 'cornu copiae' — 'horn of plenty'
• Symbol: a curved horn overflowing with fruit and vegetables

quiz.question: The philosopher described his ideal society — where there was no poverty, no crime, and everyone was equal — as a utopia. What is implied about such a place?
options: It already exists somewhere
It is an ideal but imaginary perfect society
It is a flawed but improving society
It is a historical civilization
quiz.answer: It is an ideal but imaginary perfect society
quiz.explanation:
A utopia is an imagined perfect society — ideal in every way but not real.
• utopia = an ideally perfect but unrealistic place or system
• Coined by Thomas More (1516): from Greek 'ou-topos' = 'no place'
• Opposite: dystopia — an imagined nightmarish society

quiz.question: The optometrist diagnosed Tom with myopia. Which activity would Tom struggle with most without his glasses?
options: Reading a book held in his hands
Reading a whiteboard across the room
Seeing objects close up
Distinguishing colors
quiz.answer: Reading a whiteboard across the room
quiz.explanation:
Myopia is nearsightedness — difficulty seeing things far away.
• myopia = nearsightedness; clear vision up close, blurry at a distance
• From Greek: 'myein' (to close/squint) + 'ops' (eye)
• Figuratively: 'myopic thinking' = short-sighted, lacking foresight

quiz.question: The soprano sang a famous aria from the opera. What exactly did she perform?
options: A spoken dialogue scene
An elaborate solo vocal piece
A duet with the tenor
A choral piece for the full ensemble
quiz.answer: An elaborate solo vocal piece
quiz.explanation:
An aria is a long, elaborate solo vocal composition in an opera or oratorio.
• aria = a solo song in opera, typically expressive and melodically complex
• From Italian: 'air, song'
• Distinct from recitative (speech-like singing) or chorus (group singing)

quiz.question: The scientist said the infection was caused by bacteria, not a virus. What does this mean for treatment?
options: Antiviral medication will work
Antibiotics may be effective, as bacteria respond to them
No treatment exists
Vaccination will immediately cure it
quiz.answer: Antibiotics may be effective, as bacteria respond to them
quiz.explanation:
Bacteria are single-celled microorganisms — unlike viruses, they can be targeted by antibiotics.
• bacteria = microscopic single-celled organisms (singular: bacterium)
• Antibiotics kill or inhibit bacteria; they do NOT work on viruses
• From Greek: 'bakterion' — 'small staff' (rod-like shape)

quiz.question: After crossing the finish line first, the marathon runner was overcome with euphoria. How was she feeling?
options: Exhausted and indifferent
An intense feeling of happiness and elation
Mild relief
Sad that the race was over
quiz.answer: An intense feeling of happiness and elation
quiz.explanation:
Euphoria is an intense feeling of happiness, excitement, or well-being — often beyond normal joy.
• euphoria = an extreme sense of elation and well-being
• From Greek: 'eu-' (well) + 'pherein' (to bear/carry)
• Can be triggered by achievement, love, or substances

quiz.question: The composer wrote a fantasia for piano. What should a listener expect?
options: A strictly structured sonata form
A free-form, imaginative musical improvisation
A piece for full orchestra only
A song with lyrics
quiz.answer: A free-form, imaginative musical improvisation
quiz.explanation:
A fantasia is a musical composition with an improvisatory, free structure driven by imagination.
• fantasia = a free-form musical piece following fantasy rather than strict structure
• From Italian: 'fantasy, imagination'
• Also used broadly: any imaginative, fantastical creation

quiz.question: The dentist administered analgesia before the procedure. What was the patient's experience during the treatment?
options: He felt everything clearly
He felt no pain
He fell completely unconscious
He felt only cold sensations
quiz.answer: He felt no pain
quiz.explanation:
Analgesia is the inability to feel pain while remaining conscious.
• analgesia = pain relief without loss of consciousness
• From Greek: 'an-' (without) + 'algesis' (pain)
• Distinct from anesthesia (full loss of sensation/consciousness)

quiz.question: After the accident, James had amnesia. He looked at his wife and asked, 'Who are you?' What does this tell us?
options: He was pretending not to know her
He had lost some or all of his memories
He had lost his ability to speak
He was suffering from hallucinations
quiz.answer: He had lost some or all of his memories
quiz.explanation:
Amnesia is a loss of memory — partial or complete.
• amnesia = inability to recall past memories, typically due to brain injury or trauma
• From Greek: 'a-' (without) + 'mneme' (memory)
• Two types: retrograde (forgetting the past) and anterograde (unable to form new memories)

quiz.question: When the regular army could not respond in time, the government called on the local militia. Who showed up?
options: Professional career soldiers from another country
A group of trained civilian volunteers organized for military service
The national police force
International peacekeepers
quiz.answer: A group of trained civilian volunteers organized for military service
quiz.explanation:
A militia is a military force composed of civilians, called up in emergencies rather than serving as full-time soldiers.
• militia = a non-professional citizen military force
• From Latin: 'militia' — military service
• Distinct from a standing army — militia members are not full-time soldiers

quiz.question: Despite everyone knowing the company's old system was broken, nobody changed it — they just kept doing things the old way. A consultant called this 'organizational inertia.' What did she mean?
options: The company was too innovative
The organization resisted change and kept its existing state
Employees were actively sabotaging reform
The company was growing too fast
quiz.answer: The organization resisted change and kept its existing state
quiz.explanation:
Inertia is the tendency of things (or organizations) to remain in their current state and resist change.
• inertia = resistance to change; tendency to stay as one is
• From physics: an object at rest stays at rest unless acted upon
• From Latin: 'inertia' — idleness, inactivity

quiz.question: The editor told the writer to stop obsessing over minutia and focus on the overall argument. What was the writer wasting time on?
options: The central thesis
Very small, trivial details
The word count
The formatting of the cover page only
quiz.answer: Very small, trivial details
quiz.explanation:
Minutia (plural: minutiae) refers to small, precise, or trivial details.
• minutia = a tiny or trivial detail, often one of many
• From Latin: 'minutia' — smallness
• Often used in the plural: 'the minutiae of daily life'

quiz.question: At the pub quiz, the host asked a round of trivia questions. What kind of questions were they?
options: Serious political and academic questions
Fun questions about minor, miscellaneous facts
Only questions about local history
Questions requiring expert scientific knowledge
quiz.answer: Fun questions about minor, miscellaneous facts
quiz.explanation:
Trivia refers to unimportant, minor, or miscellaneous facts and details.
• trivia = bits of minor, often entertaining, factual knowledge
• From Latin: 'trivium' — a crossroads, a public place (common, ordinary knowledge)
• 'Trivial' = of little importance

quiz.question: A child with dyslexia sits down to read a passage. What difficulty is he most likely to experience?
options: He cannot see the page due to poor eyesight
He has trouble processing written letters and words despite normal intelligence
He refuses to try reading
He can read but cannot understand spoken language
quiz.answer: He has trouble processing written letters and words despite normal intelligence
quiz.explanation:
Dyslexia is a learning difficulty that impairs the ability to read and process written language.
• dyslexia = difficulty reading, spelling, and decoding text; unrelated to intelligence
• From Greek: 'dys-' (difficulty) + 'lexis' (word/speech)
• Dyslexics often see letters reversed or jumbled

quiz.question: The doctor was concerned about a patient with anorexia who had lost dangerous amounts of weight. What is the central feature of this condition?
options: An inability to absorb nutrients due to a digestive disorder
A severe restriction of food intake driven by a distorted body image
An allergy to many types of food
A loss of the ability to taste food
quiz.answer: A severe restriction of food intake driven by a distorted body image
quiz.explanation:
Anorexia (nervosa) is an eating disorder characterized by extreme food restriction and a distorted perception of one's own body.
• anorexia = an eating disorder involving self-starvation and intense fear of weight gain
• From Greek: 'an-' (without) + 'orexis' (appetite)
• A serious and potentially life-threatening mental health condition

quiz.question: The forensic report listed asphyxia as the cause of death. What does this indicate?
options: The victim died from blood loss
The victim died from deprivation of oxygen
The victim suffered a heart attack
The victim was poisoned
quiz.answer: The victim died from deprivation of oxygen
quiz.explanation:
Asphyxia is a condition of severely deficient oxygen supply to the body, leading to unconsciousness or death.
• asphyxia = suffocation; oxygen deprivation causing death or loss of consciousness
• From Greek: 'a-' (without) + 'sphyzein' (to throb — no pulse)
• Can be caused by choking, drowning, or strangulation

quiz.question: Before leaving for the Arctic expedition, the team leader told everyone to pack a parka. What were they being asked to bring?
options: A lightweight summer jacket
A heavy, hooded waterproof coat for extreme cold
Thermal underwear
A tent for cold weather
quiz.answer: A heavy, hooded waterproof coat for extreme cold
quiz.explanation:
A parka is a large, warm, hooded jacket — originally designed for Arctic conditions.
• parka = a thick, hooded coat designed for cold weather
• Originally from the Nenets people of Siberia
• Adopted into modern fashion; synonymous with a heavy hooded coat

quiz.question: The patient recovered from meningitis but suffered a sequela: permanent hearing loss. What is a sequela?
options: A symptom of the original illness
A complication or condition that follows as a result of a prior disease
The treatment given after recovery
A relapse of the same illness
quiz.answer: A complication or condition that follows as a result of a prior disease
quiz.explanation:
A sequela (plural: sequelae) is a condition that is a consequence of a previous illness or injury.
• sequela = an aftereffect or complication resulting from a disease or injury
• From Latin: 'sequela' — a sequel, a following
• Common in medical reports: 'sequelae of COVID-19'

quiz.question: The documentary described the gorilla as the largest living primate. What kind of animal is it?
options: A large Old World monkey
The biggest great ape, found in African forests
A bear-like mammal
A large carnivorous predator
quiz.answer: The biggest great ape, found in African forests
quiz.explanation:
A gorilla is the largest of the great apes, native to the forests of central Africa.
• gorilla = a large, powerful herbivorous great ape
• From Ancient Greek via a Phoenician word meaning 'wild/hairy people'
• Close relative of humans — shares ~98.3% of DNA

quiz.question: The basketball player's shot traced a perfect parabola through the air. What shape did the ball's path make?
options: A straight line
A symmetrical curve that rises and falls
A spiral
A zigzag
quiz.answer: A symmetrical curve that rises and falls
quiz.explanation:
A parabola is a symmetrical open curve — the shape traced by a projectile in motion.
• parabola = a U-shaped curve; the path of any thrown or launched object
• From Greek: 'para-' (beside) + 'bolē' (throw)
• Key property: every point is equidistant from a fixed focus and a straight line (directrix)

quiz.question: The scientist said the formula for water is H₂O. What does this tell us?
options: The taste and color of water
The exact chemical composition of water — two hydrogen atoms bonded to one oxygen
The process used to purify water
The temperature at which water boils
quiz.answer: The exact chemical composition of water — two hydrogen atoms bonded to one oxygen
quiz.explanation:
A formula is a symbolic expression of the constituents of a compound or a rule expressed in symbols.
• formula = a fixed rule, expression, or chemical representation
• H₂O specifies exactly what atoms make up one water molecule
• From Latin: 'formula' — a small form or rule

quiz.question: Korea is often described as a peninsula. What does this tell us about its geography?
options: It is an island completely surrounded by water
It is a piece of land almost entirely surrounded by water, connected to a mainland
It is a landlocked region
It is a mountain range
quiz.answer: It is a piece of land almost entirely surrounded by water, connected to a mainland
quiz.explanation:
A peninsula is a piece of land projecting into a body of water, connected to the mainland on one side.
• peninsula = land surrounded by water on three sides
• From Latin: 'paene' (almost) + 'insula' (island) — 'almost an island'
• Examples: the Korean Peninsula, the Iberian Peninsula (Spain & Portugal)

quiz.question: The chef used a spatula to flip the pancakes in the pan. What is a spatula?
options: A sharp knife for cutting
A flat, broad utensil for lifting and flipping food
A ladle for serving soups
A grater for cheese
quiz.answer: A flat, broad utensil for lifting and flipping food
quiz.explanation:
A spatula is a flat-bladed kitchen tool used for mixing, spreading, or flipping food.
• spatula = a flat, flexible tool for cooking or spreading
• From Latin: 'spatula' — a flat piece of wood or metal
• Also used in surgery and laboratory settings

quiz.question: The critic dismissed the film as pure melodrama. What was she saying about it?
options: It had excellent musical scoring
It was overly dramatic and emotionally exaggerated
It was too subtle for most audiences
It was a documentary
quiz.answer: It was overly dramatic and emotionally exaggerated
quiz.explanation:
Melodrama refers to sensational, exaggerated drama that appeals to emotions.
• melodrama = drama with exaggerated emotions and simplistic moral conflicts
• Originally: a play with background music ('melos' = song)
• Now used critically: 'Don't be so melodramatic' = stop overreacting

quiz.question: From the mountaintop, the hikers had a panorama of the valley below. What could they see?
options: A narrow, restricted view through the trees
A complete, wide, unobstructed view in every direction
Only the path they had come up
A view of one specific landmark
quiz.answer: A complete, wide, unobstructed view in every direction
quiz.explanation:
A panorama is a wide, unobstructed view of an extensive area.
• panorama = a sweeping, all-encompassing view
• From Greek: 'pan' (all) + 'horama' (view)
• Also used figuratively: 'a panorama of 20th-century history'

quiz.question: The idea of compromising on human rights was anathema to her. What does this tell us about her attitude?
options: She found it difficult but acceptable
She found it absolutely repugnant and utterly unacceptable
She was neutral on the topic
She was considering it carefully
quiz.answer: She found it absolutely repugnant and utterly unacceptable
quiz.explanation:
Anathema means something or someone strongly detested or loathed — utterly unacceptable.
• anathema = something vehemently disliked or rejected
• Originally: a formal church curse/excommunication
• From Greek: 'anathema' — something set apart (for destruction)

quiz.question: The young priest questioned the church's dogma on the afterlife. What exactly was he questioning?
options: A recently introduced church policy
An established principle or set of beliefs held as absolute truth by the institution
His own personal faith
A hymn in the service
quiz.answer: An established principle or set of beliefs held as absolute truth by the institution
quiz.explanation:
Dogma refers to a fixed set of principles or beliefs laid down by an authority as incontrovertibly true.
• dogma = an authoritative doctrine that is not to be doubted or disputed
• From Greek: 'dogma' — opinion, decree
• 'Dogmatic' = refusing to question one's own principles

quiz.question: During the run, Rachel's asthma flared up. What symptom would you most expect her to experience?
options: Severe chest pain from her heart
Wheezing and difficulty breathing due to narrowed airways
Muscle cramps in her legs
A sudden nosebleed
quiz.answer: Wheezing and difficulty breathing due to narrowed airways
quiz.explanation:
Asthma is a chronic respiratory condition in which airways narrow and swell, making breathing difficult.
• asthma = a condition causing wheezing, breathlessness, and chest tightness
• Triggers: exercise, allergens, cold air, stress
• From Greek: 'asthma' — panting

quiz.question: The lawyer faced a dilemma: tell the truth and lose the case, or stay silent and betray her ethics. What is her situation?
options: She has a clear and obvious right choice
She is stuck between two equally difficult or undesirable options
She has too many good options to choose from
She has no options at all
quiz.answer: She is stuck between two equally difficult or undesirable options
quiz.explanation:
A dilemma is a situation requiring a choice between two undesirable alternatives.
• dilemma = a difficult choice between two problematic options
• From Greek: 'di-' (two) + 'lemma' (premise)
• Common misuse: 'dilemma' is not just any problem — it specifically involves two difficult sides

quiz.question: The teacher told the student, 'You've used a comma where a full stop is needed.' What punctuation error did the student make?
options: They used a question mark incorrectly
They used a small pause mark (,) where the sentence should have ended completely (.)
They forgot to use quotation marks
They used too many exclamation points
quiz.answer: They used a small pause mark (,) where the sentence should have ended completely (.)
quiz.explanation:
A comma (,) is a punctuation mark indicating a pause within a sentence — not a full stop.
• comma = a punctuation mark used to separate clauses or items in a list
• A full stop ends the sentence; a comma only pauses it
• Using a comma where a full stop belongs is called a 'comma splice'

quiz.question: The patient was in a coma after the accident. What does this mean about his condition?
options: He was sleeping normally and could be woken up
He was in a state of deep unconsciousness and could not be roused
He was paralyzed but fully conscious
He had temporary amnesia
quiz.answer: He was in a state of deep unconsciousness and could not be roused
quiz.explanation:
A coma is a state of prolonged deep unconsciousness from which a person cannot be woken.
• coma = a deep, prolonged state of unconsciousness
• From Greek: 'koma' — deep sleep
• Distinct from sleep: a person in a coma cannot be roused by normal stimuli

quiz.question: The baker said the aroma of fresh bread was part of the customer experience. What was she referring to?
options: The visual appearance of the bread
The pleasant smell of the bread
The texture of the crust
The price of the loaves
quiz.answer: The pleasant smell of the bread
quiz.explanation:
Aroma refers to a distinctive, typically pleasant smell.
• aroma = a distinctive, agreeable scent
• From Greek: 'aroma' — spice
• Aroma implies pleasantness — contrast with 'odor' or 'stench'

quiz.question: Victorian doctors once believed that disease was spread by miasma from swamps and rotting material. What did they think was the cause of illness?
options: Contaminated water sources
Noxious, polluted air or vapors
Contact with infected people
Bad food
quiz.answer: Noxious, polluted air or vapors
quiz.explanation:
Miasma is a highly unpleasant or unhealthy smell, historically believed to carry disease.
• miasma = foul-smelling air once thought to cause illness
• From Greek: 'miasma' — pollution, stain
• Miasma theory was replaced by germ theory in the 19th century
• Figuratively: 'a miasma of corruption' = a pervasive atmosphere of wrongdoing

quiz.question: The new CEO had tremendous charisma and quickly won over even her most skeptical employees. What quality made her so persuasive?
options: Her technical expertise in the field
A compelling personal charm that naturally attracted people to her
Her strict management style
Her high salary
quiz.answer: A compelling personal charm that naturally attracted people to her
quiz.explanation:
Charisma is a compelling personal charm and magnetism that inspires devotion or enthusiasm in others.
• charisma = a natural personal magnetism and charm
• From Greek: 'kharisma' — divine gift
• A charismatic person draws people in effortlessly

quiz.question: The scientist said the northern lights are among the most spectacular natural phenomena on Earth. What are phenomena?
options: Scientific theories
Observable facts or events in the natural world
Unexplained supernatural events only
Laboratory experiments
quiz.answer: Observable facts or events in the natural world
quiz.explanation:
Phenomena are observable events or facts — things that can be perceived by the senses.
• phenomena = plural of 'phenomenon'; observable occurrences
• From Greek: 'phainomenon' — that which appears
• Note: 'phenomena' is plural; 'phenomenon' is the singular form

quiz.question: The lawyer received a subpoena to appear in court. What must she do?
options: She may choose whether or not to attend
She is legally required to appear in court
She must submit a written statement only
She must pay a court fee
quiz.answer: She is legally required to appear in court
quiz.explanation:
A subpoena is a legal document ordering someone to appear in court or produce evidence.
• subpoena = a court order compelling attendance or production of evidence
• From Latin: 'sub poena' — 'under penalty' (failure to comply is illegal)
• Can also require producing documents (subpoena duces tecum)

quiz.question: The program listed 'Principal Ballerina' as the lead performer. What kind of performer is she?
options: A lead female classical ballet dancer
A singer in the opera
A classical musician in the orchestra
A choreographer who directs others
quiz.answer: A lead female classical ballet dancer
quiz.explanation:
A ballerina is a female ballet dancer, especially a principal dancer in a ballet company.
• ballerina = a trained female ballet dancer
• From Italian: 'ballerina' — female dancer
• 'Principal ballerina' = the top-ranked female dancer in the company

quiz.question: The antique dealer praised the old copper bowl for its beautiful patina. What was she admiring?
options: The bowl's original factory shine
A surface sheen or coloring developed through age and use
The engraving on the bottom
Its unusually heavy weight
quiz.answer: A surface sheen or coloring developed through age and use
quiz.explanation:
Patina is a sheen or coloring on a surface that develops naturally through age, use, or chemical change.
• patina = the surface appearance (often greenish or matte) acquired through aging
• From Italian/Latin: 'patina' — a shallow dish (the coating seen on old dishes)
• Figuratively: 'a patina of respectability' = an acquired, possibly superficial appearance

quiz.question: The technician climbed onto the roof to reposition the antenna. What was she adjusting and why?
options: A solar panel, to generate more electricity
A rod or wire device used for sending or receiving radio/TV signals
A weather sensor for measuring temperature
A camera for security footage
quiz.answer: A rod or wire device used for sending or receiving radio/TV signals
quiz.explanation:
An antenna is a device for transmitting or receiving electromagnetic signals such as radio or TV broadcasts.
• antenna = a metal rod or dish used to receive or transmit signals
• From Latin: 'antenna' — sail yard (the crossbar on a ship's mast)
• In biology: antennae are sensory organs on insects

quiz.question: During the solar eclipse, people in the penumbra saw only a partial eclipse. What does this tell us about where they were standing?
options: They were in the zone of total darkness
They were in the partially shaded outer region where the eclipse was incomplete
They were too far away to see any eclipse
They were directly under the moon's shadow
quiz.answer: They were in the partially shaded outer region where the eclipse was incomplete
quiz.explanation:
The penumbra is the partially shaded outer region of a shadow — where some light still reaches.
• penumbra = partial shadow; the region between full shadow and full light
• From Latin: 'paene' (almost) + 'umbra' (shadow)
• Contrast: the umbra is the zone of total shadow (full eclipse)

quiz.question: The documentary showed caribou migrating across the tundra. What kind of landscape were they crossing?
options: A dense tropical rainforest
A vast, flat, treeless Arctic plain with frozen ground
A hot, sandy desert
A deep river valley
quiz.answer: A vast, flat, treeless Arctic plain with frozen ground
quiz.explanation:
The tundra is a vast, flat, treeless Arctic or alpine region with permanently frozen subsoil.
• tundra = cold, treeless plain in Arctic or high-altitude regions
• From Russian: 'tundra' — from a Sami word for 'uplands'
• Characterized by permafrost, low vegetation, and extreme cold

quiz.question: The scientist dismissed cold fusion as a chimera that researchers have chased for decades. What was he saying about cold fusion?
options: It is a well-established scientific process
It is an unrealistic illusion or impossible dream
It is a recent breakthrough
It is a dangerous experiment
quiz.answer: It is an unrealistic illusion or impossible dream
quiz.explanation:
A chimera is an unrealistic dream, illusion, or impossible goal.
• chimera = a hope or dream that is impossible to achieve
• From Greek mythology: a fire-breathing monster with a lion's head, goat's body, and serpent's tail
• Scientific use: a chimera is also an organism with genetically distinct cells from different origins

quiz.question: The restaurant menu offered a plethora of options. What does this tell us about the menu?
options: There were very few choices
There was an overwhelming, excessive number of choices
The choices were carefully curated and limited
Choices changed daily
quiz.answer: There was an overwhelming, excessive number of choices
quiz.explanation:
A plethora means an excess or superabundance of something.
• plethora = an overabundance; more than enough, often overwhelmingly so
• From Greek: 'plethora' — fullness
• Implies the quantity may be excessive, not just generous

quiz.question: The ecologist published a study of the flora of the Amazon basin. What was the study about?
options: The animal species in the Amazon
The plant life of the Amazon
The river systems of the Amazon
The indigenous peoples of the Amazon
quiz.answer: The plant life of the Amazon
quiz.explanation:
Flora refers to the plant life of a particular region or time period.
• flora = the plants of a given area, period, or environment
• Named after Flora, the Roman goddess of flowers
• Opposite: fauna = the animal life of a region

quiz.question: The concert hall was full, but the orchestra pit was below the stage. Where were the musicians sitting?
options: On the stage in front of the audience
In the sunken area at the front of the stage
In the balcony above the audience
Behind the stage in a separate room
quiz.answer: In the sunken area at the front of the stage
quiz.explanation:
An orchestra is a large ensemble of musicians; the orchestra pit is the sunken space in front of the stage where they perform.
• orchestra = a large group of instrumentalists
• From Greek: 'orchestra' — the dancing place in a theatre
• An orchestra typically includes strings, woodwinds, brass, and percussion

quiz.question: The violinist's bravura performance earned a standing ovation. What kind of performance did she give?
options: A slow, mournful recital
A technically brilliant, dazzlingly skillful display
A simple, understated piece
A collaborative duet
quiz.answer: A technically brilliant, dazzlingly skillful display
quiz.explanation:
Bravura describes a performance displaying brilliant technique and daring skill.
• bravura = a brilliant, bold, technically impressive display of skill
• From Italian: 'bravery, skill'
• Often used for music but extends to any bold, skillful performance

quiz.question: The geologist pointed to a mesa rising above the desert floor. What were they looking at?
options: A deep canyon carved by a river
An isolated flat-topped hill with steep sides
A sand dune
A volcanic crater
quiz.answer: An isolated flat-topped hill with steep sides
quiz.explanation:
A mesa is an isolated flat-topped hill with steep sides, found in arid regions.
• mesa = a flat-topped elevation with cliff-like sides
• From Spanish: 'mesa' — table
• Common in the American Southwest; similar but smaller than a plateau

quiz.question: The program listed 'Beethoven's Piano Sonata No. 14.' What kind of piece were the audience about to hear?
options: A piece for full orchestra
A formal instrumental composition, typically in several movements
A vocal aria with piano accompaniment
An improvised solo performance
quiz.answer: A formal instrumental composition, typically in several movements
quiz.explanation:
A sonata is a composition for one or two instruments, typically in three or four movements.
• sonata = a multi-movement instrumental composition
• From Italian: 'sonata' — something played (vs. cantata = something sung)
• Beethoven's 'Moonlight' Sonata is Sonata No. 14

quiz.question: Bach's cantata for the Sunday service required a choir, soloists, and orchestra. How is a cantata performed?
options: It is played on instruments alone, with no singers
It is a vocal work sung by soloists and/or choir, usually with instrumental accompaniment
It is spoken as a dramatic monologue
It is a dance piece with no music
quiz.answer: It is a vocal work sung by soloists and/or choir, usually with instrumental accompaniment
quiz.explanation:
A cantata is a musical composition for voices and instruments in several movements.
• cantata = a vocal composition for singers and instruments
• From Italian: 'cantata' — something sung (from 'cantare' = to sing)
• Contrast with sonata (played), opera (staged), and aria (single solo song)

quiz.question: The city of New Orleans was built on the Mississippi River delta. What does this tell us about the geography of the area?
options: It is on a high cliff above the river
It is on a low, triangular deposit of sediment where the river meets the sea
It is at the river's source in the mountains
It is on an island in the middle of the river
quiz.answer: It is on a low, triangular deposit of sediment where the river meets the sea
quiz.explanation:
A river delta is the triangular area of sediment deposited where a river meets a larger body of water.
• delta = a triangular alluvial deposit at a river's mouth
• Named after the Greek letter Δ (delta), which is triangular
• Deltas are among the most fertile and densely populated areas on Earth

quiz.question: The graphic designer changed the brand color from red to magenta. What did the logo now look like?
options: A deeper, darker red
A purplish-red or pinkish-red color
A bright orange
A pale pink
quiz.answer: A purplish-red or pinkish-red color
quiz.explanation:
Magenta is a vivid purplish-red or reddish-purple color.
• magenta = a bright pinkish-purple color between red and purple
• Named after the Battle of Magenta (1859) in Italy
• One of the four CMYK printing colors (Cyan, Magenta, Yellow, Key/Black)

quiz.question: The judge said there was not one iota of evidence to support the claim. What was she saying about the evidence?
options: The evidence was compelling but flawed
There was not the tiniest, smallest amount of evidence
There was only one piece of evidence
The evidence was circumstantial
quiz.answer: There was not the tiniest, smallest amount of evidence
quiz.explanation:
An iota means an extremely small amount — the very least possible.
• iota = a tiny amount; the smallest degree
• Iota (ι) is the smallest letter in the Greek alphabet
• 'Not one iota' = absolutely nothing whatsoever

quiz.question: The sales manager told her team they had to hit their monthly quota of 50 units. What happens if they sell only 45?
options: They have exceeded expectations
They have fallen short of the required target
They are exactly on track
The quota resets immediately
quiz.answer: They have fallen short of the required target
quiz.explanation:
A quota is a fixed, required share or target amount that must be reached.
• quota = a minimum required quantity or allocated share
• From Latin: 'quota (pars)' — 'how large a part'
• Failing to meet a quota means falling below the set requirement

quiz.question: The surgeon said the aorta had been damaged in the accident. Why was this immediately life-threatening?
options: The aorta filters toxins from the blood
The aorta is the main artery carrying blood from the heart — damage causes massive blood loss
The aorta controls breathing
The aorta connects the brain to the spine
quiz.answer: The aorta is the main artery carrying blood from the heart — damage causes massive blood loss
quiz.explanation:
The aorta is the largest artery in the body, carrying oxygenated blood from the heart to the rest of the body.
• aorta = the main artery leaving the left ventricle of the heart
• Damage to the aorta is rapidly fatal due to catastrophic blood loss
• From Greek: 'aortē' — from 'aeirein' (to lift, to carry)

quiz.question: From the terrace of her villa, Maria enjoyed a stunning vista of the Tuscan hills. What was she looking at?
options: A detailed painting of the hills
A sweeping, pleasing view of the landscape
A map of the surrounding area
A narrow path leading down to the valley
quiz.answer: A sweeping, pleasing view of the landscape
quiz.explanation:
A vista is a pleasing view, especially one seen through a long, narrow opening.
• vista = a scenic view, often framed or extending into the distance
• From Italian: 'vista' — sight, view
• Also used figuratively: 'a vista of new possibilities'

quiz.question: The theater company performed an operetta rather than a full opera. What should the audience expect?
options: A very serious, tragic three-hour opera
A lighter, shorter musical work with spoken dialogue and comedy
A purely instrumental concert
A ballet with no singing
quiz.answer: A lighter, shorter musical work with spoken dialogue and comedy
quiz.explanation:
An operetta is a short, light opera with spoken dialogue and comic elements.
• operetta = a light, short opera, often comic in tone
• From Italian: 'little opera' (diminutive of 'opera')
• Examples: Gilbert and Sullivan's works; Die Fledermaus

quiz.question: Geologists warned that lava from the erupting volcano was flowing toward the town. What were residents facing?
options: A cloud of volcanic ash in the air
Molten rock flowing down the mountainside
A flood caused by melting snow
A mudslide from heavy rains
quiz.answer: Molten rock flowing down the mountainside
quiz.explanation:
Lava is molten rock expelled from a volcano during an eruption.
• lava = magma that has reached the surface; liquid rock flowing from a volcano
• From Italian dialect: 'lava' — torrential stream
• Lava destroys everything in its path and hardens into igneous rock

quiz.question: Astronomers observed a nova in a distant galaxy — briefly, it outshone everything else in that galaxy. What happened?
options: A new planet formed
A star underwent a sudden, dramatic explosion in brightness
A black hole was created
A galaxy merged with another
quiz.answer: A star underwent a sudden, dramatic explosion in brightness
quiz.explanation:
A nova is a star that suddenly increases greatly in brightness and then gradually fades.
• nova = a temporary dramatic brightening of a star due to thermonuclear explosion
• From Latin: 'nova (stella)' — new star
• Distinct from a supernova, which is far more energetic and destroys the star

quiz.question: Scientists calculated that when the massive star dies, it will become a supernova. What will happen to the star?
options: It will gradually cool and dim over thousands of years
It will explode catastrophically, briefly outshining entire galaxies, then collapse
It will split into two smaller stars
It will be absorbed by a nearby black hole
quiz.answer: It will explode catastrophically, briefly outshining entire galaxies, then collapse
quiz.explanation:
A supernova is a massive stellar explosion — one of the most energetic events in the universe.
• supernova = a catastrophic explosion of a massive star at the end of its life
• Far more powerful than a nova; can outshine an entire galaxy for weeks
• The remnant may become a neutron star or black hole

quiz.question: The butterfly was still in its larva stage. What did it look like at this point?
options: A winged, fully developed butterfly
A small, worm-like caterpillar
A hard-shelled pupa
A tiny egg
quiz.answer: A small, worm-like caterpillar
quiz.explanation:
A larva is the active, immature form of an insect before it undergoes metamorphosis.
• larva = the juvenile, worm-like stage of an insect's life cycle
• From Latin: 'larva' — ghost, mask (it 'masks' the adult form)
• A butterfly larva = a caterpillar; a fly larva = a maggot

quiz.question: The poem had four stanzas of six lines each. How was the poem arranged?
options: As one continuous block of 24 lines
As four separate groups of six lines
As six groups of four lines
As a single long sentence broken into parts
quiz.answer: As four separate groups of six lines
quiz.explanation:
A stanza is a group of lines forming a unit in a poem, like a paragraph in prose.
• stanza = a grouped set of lines in a poem
• From Italian: 'stanza' — room, stopping place
• Types: couplet (2 lines), quatrain (4), sestet (6), octet (8)

quiz.question: Tourists gathered in the piazza in front of the cathedral. Where exactly were they?
options: Inside the cathedral's main hall
In an open public square in front of the cathedral
In a rooftop café above the square
In a covered market nearby
quiz.answer: In an open public square in front of the cathedral
quiz.explanation:
A piazza is a public square or marketplace, especially in an Italian town.
• piazza = an open public square, especially in Italy
• From Italian, related to the French 'place' and English 'place'
• Examples: Piazza San Marco in Venice, Piazza Navona in Rome

quiz.question: The nurse told the child not to pick the scab on his knee. What was she protecting?
options: A bandage she had applied
The dry, hardened crust of dried blood forming over the healing wound
New stitches in the cut
A skin graft
quiz.answer: The dry, hardened crust of dried blood forming over the healing wound
quiz.explanation:
A scab is the hard, dry crust that forms over a wound as it heals.
• scab = a protective crust of dried blood and serum over a healing wound
• Picking it off delays healing and can cause scarring
• Also used figuratively for a strike-breaker (informal, derogatory)

quiz.question: The mason laid a slab of granite on the kitchen counter. What did he put down?
options: A thin sheet of paper-like material
A thick, flat, broad piece of stone
A bag of loose gravel
A metal frame
quiz.answer: A thick, flat, broad piece of stone
quiz.explanation:
A slab is a large, thick, flat piece of a hard material such as stone, concrete, or wood.
• slab = a broad, flat, thick piece of material
• Also: a slab of chocolate, a slab of concrete
• Implies weight and solidity

quiz.question: The interior designer said the office was drab and needed a makeover. What was wrong with the office?
options: It was cluttered with too much furniture
It was dull, colorless, and lacking in any visual interest
It was too brightly lit
It was too small for the staff
quiz.answer: It was dull, colorless, and lacking in any visual interest
quiz.explanation:
Drab describes something that is dull, lacking brightness or interest — monotonously gray or colorless.
• drab = lacking color, excitement, or visual appeal
• Often implies a depressing or spirit-sapping quality
• Antonyms: vivid, vibrant, colorful

quiz.question: She felt a stab of guilt when she heard the news. What did she experience?
options: A physical injury to her body
A sudden, sharp feeling of guilt
A prolonged sense of shame that lasted for weeks
A mild, vague feeling of regret
quiz.answer: A sudden, sharp feeling of guilt
quiz.explanation:
A stab (as a feeling) means a sudden, sharp pang of emotion.
• stab = a sudden, piercing sensation (physical or emotional)
• The metaphor compares the sharp onset of guilt to the sharp entry of a blade
• 'A stab of pain, jealousy, regret' — always sudden and intense

quiz.question: The actor forgot his lines and had to adlib the rest of the scene. What did he do?
options: He stopped the performance and asked for the script
He improvised dialogue spontaneously, without a script
He repeated his last memorized line several times
He stayed silent until prompted by another actor
quiz.answer: He improvised dialogue spontaneously, without a script
quiz.explanation:
To adlib means to improvise speech or actions without preparation or a script.
• adlib = to improvise; to speak or perform without prepared material
• From Latin: 'ad libitum' — 'at one's pleasure, freely'
• Used as a verb, noun, and adjective: 'an adlib remark'

quiz.question: The politician gave a glib answer to the journalist's difficult question about poverty. What was wrong with his answer?
options: It was too long and detailed
It was fluent and confident but superficial and insincere
It was too technical for most people
It was emotionally moving and deeply felt
quiz.answer: It was fluent and confident but superficial and insincere
quiz.explanation:
Glib describes something said fluently and confidently but without depth or genuine concern.
• glib = slick and smooth in speech, lacking sincerity or substance
• Often implies the speaker is not taking the issue seriously
• 'A glib response' sidesteps the real difficulty

quiz.question: The calligrapher replaced the nib on her pen before continuing her work. What did she replace?
options: The ink cartridge inside the pen
The metal tip of the pen that touches the paper and controls ink flow
The barrel (body) of the pen
The cap of the pen
quiz.answer: The metal tip of the pen that touches the paper and controls ink flow
quiz.explanation:
A nib is the metal writing tip of a fountain pen or dip pen.
• nib = the pointed metal tip of a pen through which ink flows
• Different nib widths produce different line thicknesses
• From Old English: 'nib' — beak, tip

quiz.question: The carpenter said the door jamb was warped and needed replacing. What part of the door was damaged?
options: The door itself
The vertical side posts of the door frame
The door handle and lock
The hinge at the top of the door
quiz.answer: The vertical side posts of the door frame
quiz.explanation:
A jamb is the vertical side post or surface of a door or window frame.
• jamb = the side post forming part of a door or window frame
• From Old French: 'jambe' — leg
• Both sides of a door opening are called jambs; the top is the lintel

quiz.question: The surgeon said the patient was lucky to keep the limb after the accident. What part of the body were they discussing?
options: An internal organ
An arm or leg
The spine
The skull
quiz.answer: An arm or leg
quiz.explanation:
A limb is an arm, leg, or wing — a projecting appendage of the body.
• limb = an arm or leg (in humans); also a branch of a tree
• 'Going out on a limb' = taking a risk (from a tree branch that might snap)
• From Old English: 'lim'

quiz.question: Despite the fire alarm going off mid-presentation, the speaker continued with perfect aplomb. How did he handle the situation?
options: He panicked and ran out of the room
He handled it with calm self-confidence and composure
He stopped and refused to continue
He made several errors due to nervousness
quiz.answer: He handled it with calm self-confidence and composure
quiz.explanation:
Aplomb means self-confident composure and poise, especially in a difficult situation.
• aplomb = calm, assured composure under pressure
• From French: 'aplomb' — perpendicularity (the idea of being perfectly upright/balanced)
• 'With aplomb' = smoothly, confidently, without being thrown off

quiz.question: Despite months of fighting the illness, the elderly patient finally succumbed. What happened?
options: He recovered completely
He died after being unable to resist the illness any longer
He was discharged from hospital
He began a new treatment
quiz.answer: He died after being unable to resist the illness any longer
quiz.explanation:
To succumb means to fail to resist a force or pressure — often meaning death in medical contexts.
• succumb = to give way to something overwhelming; to yield or die
• From Latin: 'succumbere' — to lie under, yield
• 'Succumb to temptation' (yield) or 'succumb to an illness' (die)

quiz.question: After sitting on the hard bench for two hours, Tom's legs felt numb. What did this mean?
options: His legs felt painfully cramped
His legs had lost feeling and sensation
His legs felt unusually warm
His legs were shaking uncontrollably
quiz.answer: His legs had lost feeling and sensation
quiz.explanation:
Numb means deprived of feeling or sensation — physically or emotionally.
• numb = having lost the ability to feel; without sensation
• Physically: caused by cold, pressure, or nerve damage
• Emotionally: 'numb with grief' = too shocked to feel anything
• From Old English: 'niman' — to take (sensation is 'taken away')

quiz.question: The child ate corn on the cob at the barbecue. What exactly did she eat?
options: Corn kernels served in a bowl
Whole ears of corn still on the central stalk, eaten directly
Cornbread baked in a cob shape
Canned sweet corn
quiz.answer: Whole ears of corn still on the central stalk, eaten directly
quiz.explanation:
A cob is the central cylindrical core of an ear of corn — eating 'on the cob' means eating the kernels directly off this core.
• cob = the hard inner core of an ear of maize/corn
• 'Corn on the cob' = corn eaten directly from the cob, not cut off
• Also: a type of sturdy horse; a rounded lump of coal

quiz.question: At the wine tasting, Gerald refused to try any wine under £50 and wrinkled his nose at anything from outside France. His friend called him a snob. What does this mean?
options: He was an expert sommelier
He had an arrogant belief that only expensive or prestigious things were worth his attention
He was very knowledgeable about wine regions
He was simply being careful with his health
quiz.answer: He had an arrogant belief that only expensive or prestigious things were worth his attention
quiz.explanation:
A snob is someone who believes they have superior taste and looks down on those they consider inferior.
• snob = a person who is condescending about others' social status, taste, or background
• Origin uncertain; possibly from cobbler's apprentice (someone of low rank mimicking high society)
• 'Wine snob', 'intellectual snob', 'food snob' — dismissive of anything 'below' their standards

quiz.question: In his speech, the senator directed a sharp barb at his opponent's financial record. What did he do?
options: He praised his opponent's transparency
He made a pointed, cutting remark intended to sting
He asked a genuine question about policy
He withdrew a previous criticism
quiz.answer: He made a pointed, cutting remark intended to sting
quiz.explanation:
A barb is a sharp, hurtful remark — like the backward-pointing spike on a fishhook or wire.
• barb = a deliberately hurtful or cutting comment
• From the literal barb: the backward-pointing spike that makes a hook hard to remove
• 'His words contained a barb' = they were designed to wound and stick
